eraDescription: F1 is established with front-engined grand prix cars derived from pre-war designs. The era was dominated by legendary drivers like Juan Manuel Fangio driving legendary machinery.

cars.name: Alfa Romeo 158
cars.description: The Alfa Romeo 158, affectionately known as the 'Alfetta', dominated the very first Formula 1 World Championship season in 1950. Its supercharged engine was incredibly powerful for its time.
cars.funFact: Despite its dominance, the car only managed about 1.5 miles per gallon of fuel, requiring massive fuel tanks.

cars.name: Alfa Romeo 159
cars.description: An evolution of the 158, the 159 was even more powerful and carried Fangio to his first world championship. The massive power output meant it was notoriously thirsty for fuel.
cars.funFact: It required up to three pit stops for fuel in a single race due to its incredibly high fuel consumption.

cars.name: Ferrari 500
cars.description: Built to Formula 2 regulations when the World Championship adopted them, the Ferrari 500 was remarkably reliable and handled beautifully thanks to its lightweight 4-cylinder engine. Ascari drove this to consecutive titles.
cars.funFact: Alberto Ascari won nine consecutive championship races in this car, a record that stood for decades.

cars.name: Maserati 250F
cars.description: Widely considered one of the most beautiful and iconic front-engined Formula 1 cars ever raced. It was the car that Fangio used to secure his fifth world championship in 1957. Beloved by drivers for its predictable handling.
cars.funFact: Fangio's drive in the 250F at the 1957 German Grand Prix is often cited as the greatest drive in F1 history.

cars.name: Mercedes-Benz W196
cars.description: The W196 marked Mercedes' successful return to F1. It featured advanced technologies like desmodromic valves and fuel injection derived from the Messerschmitt fighter plane. It won 9 of the 12 races it entered.
cars.funFact: There were two versions of the W196: an open-wheel version and a streamlined 'Type Monza' for high-speed tracks.

cars.name: Lancia D50
cars.description: The D50 was highly innovative, featuring an engine used as a stressed chassis member and twin side-mounted fuel tanks for better weight distribution. Ferrari took over the project after Lancia's financial collapse.
cars.funFact: The side-mounted pannier fuel tanks improved aerodynamics but made the car dangerously volatile in a crash.

cars.name: Vanwall VW57
cars.description: A pioneering British car designed with aerodynamics in mind by Frank Costin. It became the first car to win the Formula One Constructors' Championship in 1958. Its sleek teardrop body made it very fast on the straights.
cars.funFact: The Vanwall engine was essentially made by combining four single-cylinder Norton motorcycle engines.

cars.name: BRM P25
cars.description: The P25 finally brought success to the British Racing Motors project after years of failure. It was incredibly light and nimble, rewarding smooth driving styles, securing BRM's first ever victory at Zandvoort.
cars.funFact: The single rear disc brake was mounted on the transmission rather than at the wheels to reduce unsprung weight.

cars.name: Cooper T51
cars.description: The Cooper T51 changed F1 forever by proving that a rear-mounted engine was vastly superior. It won the 1959 championship, making front-engined cars obsolete almost overnight. The design allowed for a smaller frontal area and better traction.
cars.funFact: Jack Brabham famously ran out of fuel at the US Grand Prix and pushed his T51 over the finish line to secure the title.

cars.name: Ferrari 246 F1
cars.description: The 246 was the last front-engined car to win a Formula 1 World Championship. Powered by the Dino V6 engine, it was robust and fast, allowing Mike Hawthorn to beat the lighter, rear-engined Coopers to the 1958 title.
cars.funFact: It was the first Formula 1 car to use a V6 engine, a configuration that is standard today.

eraDescription: Cooper pioneers the rear-engine layout, making front-engined cars obsolete almost overnight. Lotus introduces the monocoque chassis, making cars much lighter and stiffer.

cars.name: Cooper T53
cars.description: The T53 'Lowline' refined the rear-engine concept, featuring lower bodywork and coil spring suspension all around. It completely dominated the 1960 season, proving the rear-engine revolution was permanent.
cars.funFact: Brabham won five consecutive races in the T53 on his way to his second World Championship.

cars.name: Lotus 25
cars.description: The Lotus 25 was the first F1 car to use a fully stressed monocoque chassis, replacing traditional tubular spaceframes. This made it radically stiffer and lighter. Jim Clark drove it to unparalleled success in 1963.
cars.funFact: Colin Chapman sketched the revolutionary monocoque chassis design on a napkin while having lunch.

cars.name: BRM P57
cars.description: The P57 brought BRM its only World Championship with Graham Hill. It was characterized by its distinctive stacked exhaust pipes and highly reliable V8 engine, which finally paid off for the long-struggling British team.
cars.funFact: It was famous for its 'stackpipe' exhausts which looked like organ pipes pointing upwards at the rear.

cars.name: Ferrari 156
cars.description: Nicknamed the 'Sharknose' for its distinctive twin-nostril front air intakes, the 156 dominated the first year of the 1.5L regulations. Phil Hill became the first American World Champion driving this beautiful but tragic car.
cars.funFact: Enzo Ferrari controversially ordered all 156 chassis to be destroyed at the end of the 1962 season; no originals exist today.

cars.name: Lotus 33
cars.description: An evolution of the 25, the Lotus 33 featured improved suspension to accommodate wider tires. Jim Clark's 1965 season in this car is legendary, winning six of the ten races and securing the championship early.
cars.funFact: Jim Clark missed the Monaco Grand Prix that year to win the Indy 500, but still easily won the F1 championship.

cars.name: Brabham BT19
cars.description: The BT19 powered Jack Brabham to his third World Championship. It was the first and only time a driver has won the championship in a car bearing his own name. It utilized a highly reliable Repco V8 engine based on an Oldsmobile block.
cars.funFact: The engine block was actually derived from a production Oldsmobile F-85 passenger car engine.

cars.name: Honda RA272
cars.description: The RA272 was the first Japanese car to win in Formula 1. Its transversely mounted, high-revving V12 engine was a marvel of engineering, sounding more like a motorcycle than a traditional car.
cars.funFact: The V12 engine could safely rev to an incredible 12,000 RPM, which was virtually unheard of in 1965.

cars.name: Eagle Mk1
cars.description: Considered by many to be the most beautiful F1 car ever constructed. Powered by a Weslake V12, Dan Gurney drove it to victory at Spa-Francorchamps, becoming the only American to win in a car of his own construction.
cars.funFact: The exhaust pipes were formed from exotic titanium, which turned brilliant shades of blue and purple when heated.

cars.name: Ferrari 312
cars.description: The 312 was Ferrari's answer to the new 3.0L regulations. Its screaming V12 sounded spectacular but the car was often heavy and plagued by team politics. It featured beautiful 'spaghetti' exhaust headers.
cars.funFact: The exhaust manifold was so complex it was known as the 'spaghetti exhaust' due to its tangled tubular appearance.

cars.name: Lotus 49 (Early)
cars.description: The 49 revolutionized F1 by using the engine as a fully stressed chassis member. It debuted the legendary Ford Cosworth DFV engine, completely changing the landscape of F1 powertrain design. Initially raced without wings.
cars.funFact: Jim Clark won the very first race the Lotus 49 and the Cosworth DFV engine ever entered at Zandvoort.

eraDescription: Inverted wings are introduced to produce aerodynamic downforce, dramatically increasing cornering speeds. The Ford-Cosworth DFV V8 engine becomes the standard power unit for most teams on the grid.

cars.name: Lotus 49B
cars.description: The 49B introduced the famous Gold Leaf livery, marking the beginning of commercial sponsorship in F1. More importantly, it was one of the first cars to successfully utilize aerodynamic wings to generate downforce.
cars.funFact: Early versions had high-strut wings mounted directly to the suspension, which were soon banned for being dangerously fragile.

cars.name: Matra MS80
cars.description: Driven brilliantly by Jackie Stewart, the Matra MS80 featured aircraft-style structural fuel tanks. It dominated the 1969 season, taking Stewart to his first of three World Championships.
cars.funFact: It is the only car built in France to win the Formula 1 World Constructors' Championship.

cars.name: Lotus 72
cars.description: A legendary wedge-shaped car that moved the cooling radiators from the nose to the sidepods, improving weight distribution and aero. Sporting the iconic John Player Special livery, it was successful across six seasons.
cars.funFact: Jochen Rindt won the 1970 title posthumously in this car, the only driver to ever do so.

cars.name: Tyrrell 003
cars.description: The 003 was designed by Derek Gardner and carried Jackie Stewart to his second World Championship. Its distinctive hammerhead nose and blunt shape housed highly effective aerodynamics and a very reliable DFV engine.
cars.funFact: The chassis was essentially a modified version of Tyrrell's very first car, the 001, built secretly in a shed.

cars.name: McLaren M23
cars.description: The M23 was a workhorse for McLaren, winning championships for Fittipaldi in '74 and Hunt in '76. It perfected the wedge shape pioneered by Lotus and remained competitive for over four years.
cars.funFact: The famous Marlboro red and white chevron livery debuted on this car, becoming synonymous with McLaren for two decades.

cars.name: Ferrari 312T
cars.description: The 'T' stood for 'Transversale', meaning the gearbox was mounted sideways. This greatly improved weight distribution and handling. Niki Lauda used it to break Ferrari's 11-year championship drought.
cars.funFact: The transverse gearbox made the car incredibly nimble, allowing Lauda to slide the car predictably through fast corners.

cars.name: Brabham BT44
cars.description: Designed by Gordon Murray, the BT44 featured clean, pyramidal lines and side-mounted radiators. Its sharp aerodynamic profile made it very fast, though occasionally let down by reliability.
cars.funFact: Its striking 'Martini Racing' livery is widely considered one of the best-looking in motorsport history.

cars.name: Tyrrell P34
cars.description: The P34 is the famous 'six-wheeler'. Using four smaller 10-inch front wheels reduced aerodynamic drag while maintaining the contact patch for grip. It actually won the 1976 Swedish Grand Prix.
cars.funFact: Goodyear eventually stopped developing the special tiny front tires, rendering the car uncompetitive.

cars.name: March 711
cars.description: Known for its bizarre elevated front wing, nicknamed the 'Tea Tray' or 'Spitfire wing'. Despite its strange appearance, Ronnie Peterson drove it aggressively to finish second in the 1971 championship.
cars.funFact: The elevated front wing was designed by Frank Costin, an aerodynamicist from the aviation industry.

cars.name: Shadow DN5
cars.description: The DN5 was blisteringly fast in qualifying, taking numerous pole positions, but suffered from terrible reliability. Its sinister all-black livery perfectly matched the enigmatic Shadow team brand.
cars.funFact: The team was backed by Don Nichols, a mysterious American figure rumored to have ties to military intelligence.

eraDescription: Teams exploit under-car Venturi tunnels to pull cars to the track using ground effects. High-boost turbochargers push engine outputs past 1,000 horsepower in qualifying trims.

cars.name: Lotus 78
cars.description: The Lotus 78 was the first F1 car to effectively harness ground effect aerodynamics. Inverted wing profiles inside the sidepods created a low-pressure area that sucked the car to the track, revolutionizing cornering speeds.
cars.funFact: Engineers kept the sidepod skirts secret by claiming they were just there to keep dirt out of the radiators.

cars.name: Lotus 79
cars.description: Known as 'Black Beauty', the Lotus 79 perfected the ground effect concept introduced by the 78. The clean aerodynamics and massive underbody downforce allowed Mario Andretti to dominate the 1978 championship.
cars.funFact: The car generated so much downforce that it often overwhelmed and broke its own suspension components.

cars.name: Brabham BT46B
cars.description: The infamous 'Fan Car' featured a massive rear fan that sucked air from beneath the car, creating incredible downforce even at a standstill. It won its debut race before being voluntarily withdrawn to avoid a ban.
cars.funFact: Niki Lauda had to avoid accelerating while stationary, or the massive suction would pull the car down onto the tarmac.

cars.name: Williams FW07
cars.description: The FW07 mastered ground-effect aerodynamics without the complexity of the Lotus cars. It brought Williams their first World Championship in 1980, dominating the grid with its simple, stiff, and brilliant design.
cars.funFact: The car was so physically demanding due to the high cornering speeds that drivers often struggled to breathe.

cars.name: Renault RE20
cars.description: Renault pioneered the 1.5L turbocharged engine. Early iterations were incredibly fast but violently unreliable, earning them the nickname 'Yellow Teapots'. The RE20 proved turbo power was the inevitable future of F1.
cars.funFact: Turbo lag was so severe drivers had to press the throttle seconds before the actual corner exit.

cars.name: Ferrari 126C2
cars.description: A brutally fast and capable ground-effect turbo car that brought Ferrari the 1982 Constructors' Championship. Sadly, it is also remembered as the car in which the legendary Gilles Villeneuve tragically lost his life.
cars.funFact: The chassis was the first Ferrari to be designed primarily by an English specialist rather than in-house Italian engineers.

cars.name: Brabham BT52
cars.description: Following the ban on ground-effect skirts, Gordon Murray designed the dart-shaped BT52 with most of its weight at the rear for traction. Nelson Piquet used its immensely powerful BMW turbo to win the 1983 title.
cars.funFact: In qualifying trim, the 1.5L BMW engine could be cranked up to produce over 1,000 horsepower.

cars.name: McLaren MP4/2
cars.description: The MP4/2 featured the first carbon fiber monocoque chassis and was powered by a highly efficient TAG-Porsche turbo engine. It dominated the mid-80s, winning three consecutive drivers' championships.
cars.funFact: Niki Lauda beat his teammate Alain Prost to the 1984 championship by just half a point in this car.

cars.name: Williams FW11
cars.description: The FW11 was powered by an ultra-reliable and immensely powerful Honda turbo engine. It won the Constructors' Championship in '86 and '87, though internal team rivalry often cost them driver titles.
cars.funFact: Nelson Piquet famously executed a legendary four-wheel drift pass on Ayrton Senna in this car at the 1986 Hungarian GP.

cars.name: McLaren MP4/4
cars.description: Arguably the most dominant Formula 1 car of all time, winning 15 out of 16 races in the 1988 season. It perfectly packaged a highly efficient Honda turbo engine into a beautifully low and sleek chassis designed by Gordon Murray.
cars.funFact: The driving position was so low that drivers had to lie almost flat on their backs to fit inside the cockpit.

eraDescription: Naturally aspirated 3.5L and later 3.0L V10 engines produce legendary high-pitched screams and extreme power curves. Electronic driver aids like traction control and active suspension are briefly introduced and then restricted.

cars.name: McLaren MP4/5
cars.description: The MP4/5 marked the return to naturally aspirated engines. Powered by a screaming Honda V10, it maintained McLaren's dominance. The car is famous for the bitter rivalry and collision between Senna and Prost at Suzuka.
cars.funFact: Honda engineers designed the V10 engine primarily because it sounded better and revved higher than a V8.

cars.name: Williams FW14B
cars.description: Often called the most technologically advanced F1 car ever built. It featured active suspension, traction control, and anti-lock brakes. It completely dominated the 1992 season with Nigel Mansell at the wheel.
cars.funFact: The active suspension required so much computing power that the car carried a heavy early-90s desktop computer inside.

cars.name: Benetton B194
cars.description: The B194 carried a young Michael Schumacher to his first World Championship in a highly controversial 1994 season. The car was incredibly nimble and featured an aerodynamically advanced high nose.
cars.funFact: Rivals heavily suspected the B194 of using illegal traction control software hidden deep in its code.

cars.name: Williams FW18
cars.description: Designed by Adrian Newey, the FW18 was aerodynamically flawless and heavily capitalized on the reliable Renault V10 engine. Damon Hill won the 1996 championship, winning 12 of the 16 races for Williams.
cars.funFact: The cockpit was designed to sit the driver as low as possible to lower the center of gravity and improve airflow to the rear wing.

cars.name: McLaren MP4/13
cars.description: The MP4/13 exploited new narrow-track regulations perfectly. With Adrian Newey aerodynamics and a powerful Mercedes engine, Mika Häkkinen used its blazing speed to defeat Michael Schumacher for the title.
cars.funFact: The team secretly developed a 'fiddle brake' steering pedal that braked individual rear wheels to eliminate understeer.

cars.name: Ferrari F2002
cars.description: Widely considered one of the greatest F1 cars ever made. It was so fast and reliable that Schumacher finished every single race on the podium in 2002. Its seamless gearbox and sculpted aero set the benchmark for years.
cars.funFact: The car was so successful it continued to be used and win races at the start of the following 2003 season.

cars.name: Williams FW26
cars.description: The FW26 featured a radical 'walrus nose' design aimed at maximizing airflow under the chassis. Combined with a monstrous BMW V10, it possessed staggering straight-line speed but was aerodynamically sensitive.
cars.funFact: Juan Pablo Montoya set what was then the fastest qualifying lap in F1 history at Monza, averaging over 161 mph.

cars.name: Ferrari F2004
cars.description: The pinnacle of the V10 era. The F2004 broke lap records at nearly every circuit it visited, many of which stood for over 15 years. Schumacher won 13 races, securing his seventh and final World Championship.
cars.funFact: Engineers originally thought their wind tunnel data was broken because the F2004 generated so much unprecedented downforce.

cars.name: Renault R25
cars.description: The R25 ended Ferrari's five-year reign and brought Fernando Alonso his first title. It was famous for its 'mass damper' suspension system which smoothed out bumps. Its screaming V10 produced a legendary sound.
cars.funFact: Fernando Alonso demonstrated the car years later in 2020, and the roaring V10 sound completely captivated the modern paddock.

cars.name: McLaren MP4-20
cars.description: Universally acknowledged as the fastest car of the 2005 season, the MP4-20 featured striking 'horn' winglets. However, its terrible engine reliability cost Kimi Räikkönen the championship despite him winning 7 races.
cars.funFact: The car was so fragile that Räikkönen repeatedly suffered engine failures while comfortably leading races.

eraDescription: Formula 1 mandates 2.4L V8 engines limited to high RPMs to reduce costs and speeds. Kinetic energy recovery systems (KERS) and drag reduction systems (DRS) make their debut.

cars.name: Renault R26
cars.description: Adapted perfectly to the new V8 regulations, the R26 successfully defended Alonso's title against a resurgent Ferrari. It heavily utilized the tuned mass damper system in the nose until the FIA controversially banned it mid-season.
cars.funFact: Renault's mastery of the rear-ward weight bias made the R26 incredibly fast off the starting line.

cars.name: McLaren MP4-23
cars.description: A highly evolved aerodynamic marvel covered in complex winglets and bargeboards. Lewis Hamilton drove it to his dramatic first World Championship victory on the very last corner of the last lap in Brazil.
cars.funFact: The car featured some of the most complex, fractal-like aerodynamic appendages ever seen before they were banned in 2009.

cars.name: Ferrari F2007
cars.description: The F2007 featured a slightly longer wheelbase for aerodynamic stability. Kimi Räikkönen staged a miraculous comeback in the second half of the season to win the championship by a single point in this car.
cars.funFact: It was the last Ferrari car to win a Formula 1 Drivers' World Championship.

cars.name: Brawn BGP 001
cars.description: A true fairy-tale car, rising from the ashes of the folded Honda team. It controversially utilized a 'double diffuser' that provided a massive aerodynamic advantage. Button won six of the first seven races.
cars.funFact: The team was bought for just £1 weeks before the season, and the car initially had no sponsors on its bright white livery.

cars.name: Red Bull RB6
cars.description: Designed by Adrian Newey, the RB6 utilized exhaust-blown diffusers to direct hot exhaust gases over the floor, generating massive downforce. It secured Vettel his first world title.
cars.funFact: Mark Webber claimed the car was so planted it felt like it could drive upside down in a tunnel.

cars.name: McLaren MP4-26
cars.description: The MP4-26 featured highly distinctive L-shaped sidepods designed to feed clean air to the rear wing and exhaust area. It was the only car able to consistently challenge the dominant Red Bulls in 2011.
cars.funFact: The car was first revealed to the public during a stunt where mechanics assembled it in a public square in Berlin.

cars.name: Ferrari 150° Italia
cars.description: Built to celebrate the 150th anniversary of the unification of Italy. Though structurally conservative, Fernando Alonso wrestled the car to a remarkable victory at Silverstone through sheer driving talent.
cars.funFact: Ford sued Ferrari over the original 'F150' name, claiming it infringed on their pickup truck trademark, leading to a rename.

cars.name: Red Bull RB9
cars.description: The absolute pinnacle of exhaust-blown aerodynamics. Sebastian Vettel won an incredible 9 consecutive races to end the season, securing his fourth straight title. The car had an almost magical ability to corner on rails.
cars.funFact: Vettel named his chassis 'Hungry Heidi' and famously performed donuts on the pit straight in India after winning the title.

cars.name: Lotus E20
cars.description: The E20 was remarkably kind to its Pirelli tires, allowing Räikkönen to execute brilliant alternative strategies. He famously drove it to victory in Abu Dhabi, telling his engineer 'Leave me alone, I know what I'm doing'.
cars.funFact: The steering wheel featured an asymmetric layout specifically customized to Kimi Räikkönen's unique driving preferences.

cars.name: Sauber C31
cars.description: The C31 was a midfield giant-killer. Its incredibly efficient aerodynamics and gentle tire usage allowed Sergio Pérez to challenge for victories and secure three spectacular podiums on a fraction of the top teams' budgets.
cars.funFact: Kamui Kobayashi secured an emotional podium finish at his home race in Suzuka driving this car.

eraDescription: The sport introduces 1.6L turbocharged hybrid power units with sophisticated heat and kinetic energy recovery systems. Mercedes dominates the early years of this highly efficient, high-torque era.

cars.name: Mercedes W05 Hybrid
cars.description: The W05 ushered in the hybrid era with a fundamentally superior split-turbo power unit design. It completely obliterated the opposition, winning 16 of 19 races and securing Hamilton his second championship.
cars.funFact: Mercedes brilliantly split the turbocharger's turbine and compressor to opposite sides of the engine, massively reducing heat and lag.

cars.name: Red Bull RB10
cars.description: Despite a heavily underpowered and unreliable Renault engine, Adrian Newey's chassis was arguably the best on the grid aerodynamically. Daniel Ricciardo managed to steal three brilliant victories against the dominant Mercedes.
cars.funFact: The car's aggressive low-drag setup allowed Ricciardo to execute his famous late-braking divebomb overtakes.

cars.name: Mercedes W07 Hybrid
cars.description: Statistically one of the most dominant cars in motorsport history, winning 19 of 21 races. Nico Rosberg and Lewis Hamilton engaged in a fierce intra-team battle driving this legendary machine.
cars.funFact: The W07 spent roughly 85% of all racing laps in the 2016 season leading the pack.

cars.name: Ferrari SF70H
cars.description: The SF70H exploited the new wider, faster aero regulations perfectly. It featured highly innovative complex sidepod intakes that the rest of the grid eventually copied. Vettel fought closely for the title before a late-season collapse.
cars.funFact: The complex sidepod design helped cool the engine efficiently while directing clean air over the floor of the car.

cars.name: Mercedes W11 EQ Performance
cars.description: Widely considered the fastest Formula 1 car ever built. It generated so much downforce it broke track records everywhere. It famously featured DAS (Dual Axis Steering), allowing the driver to change tire toe-angle on the straights.
cars.funFact: The team switched from their traditional silver to an all-black livery just before the season to support diversity and anti-racism.

cars.name: Red Bull RB16B
cars.description: The RB16B finally provided Verstappen with the machinery to fight Mercedes. Powered by a potent Honda engine, it featured extremely tight packaging to claw back downforce lost by regulation changes.
cars.funFact: To celebrate their engine partnership, the car ran a special white livery in Turkey as a tribute to Honda's first winning F1 car.

cars.name: McLaren MCL35M
cars.description: The papaya orange MCL35M adapted to a new Mercedes engine perfectly. It was blisteringly fast in a straight line, allowing Daniel Ricciardo to lead home a spectacular 1-2 finish at Monza.
cars.funFact: It featured a distinct matte finish that saved a tiny amount of weight compared to traditional gloss paint.

cars.name: Ferrari SF90
cars.description: Possessed a famously powerful engine that gave it unmatched straight-line speed. Young star Charles Leclerc used this advantage to claim memorable back-to-back victories at Spa and Monza.
cars.funFact: The SF90's engine advantage was later subject to a highly secretive FIA investigation resulting in a private settlement.

cars.name: Racing Point RP20
cars.description: Controversially dubbed the 'Pink Mercedes' because it was essentially a direct reverse-engineered clone of the 2019 championship-winning Mercedes W10. Sergio Pérez drove it to a stunning last-to-first victory in Sakhir.
cars.funFact: The team was fined 400,000 Euros and docked 15 points for illegally copying the Mercedes rear brake ducts.

cars.name: AlphaTauri AT01
cars.description: Sporting arguably the best livery on the grid, the AT01 was a highly effective midfield car. Pierre Gasly capitalized on a chaotic race at Monza to secure a wildly popular shock victory for the junior Red Bull team.
cars.funFact: The team changed its name from Toro Rosso to AlphaTauri to promote Red Bull's new fashion brand.

eraDescription: The regulations are overhauled to bring back ground effect aerodynamics to improve close racing and overtaking. Over-wheel winglets and simplified front wings give cars a futuristic look.

cars.name: Red Bull RB18
cars.description: The RB18 crushed the competition during the dawn of the ground effect era. Its exceptional aerodynamic efficiency provided high top speeds without sacrificing cornering grip, avoiding the 'porpoising' issues that plagued others.
cars.funFact: Max Verstappen won 15 races in 2022, breaking the record for most wins in a single season at the time.

cars.name: Ferrari F1-75
cars.description: The F1-75 featured a stunning, aggressive design with unique 'bathtub' style sidepods. It was blisteringly fast in qualifying but was hampered by reliability and strategy woes during races.
cars.funFact: The car's deep red paint was explicitly chosen to celebrate Ferrari's 75th anniversary as a car manufacturer.

cars.name: Mercedes W13
cars.description: The W13 debuted a radical 'zero-pod' design, completely shrinking the sidepods. However, the car suffered violently from aerodynamic bouncing (porpoising), making it incredibly painful for the drivers to race.
cars.funFact: The bouncing was so severe at Baku that Lewis Hamilton struggled to climb out of the cockpit after the race due to back pain.

cars.name: Red Bull RB19
cars.description: The RB19 is statistically the most dominant car in F1 history, winning 21 of the 22 races in 2023. It perfected the RB18's philosophy, being light, perfectly balanced, and devastatingly fast in race trim.
cars.funFact: Max Verstappen won 10 consecutive races in this car, breaking Sebastian Vettel's long-standing record.

cars.name: Aston Martin AMR23
cars.description: The AMR23 was the surprise of the 2023 season, jumping from the midfield to become Red Bull's closest challenger early on. A 41-year-old Fernando Alonso drove it to 8 spectacular podium finishes.
cars.funFact: The car heavily featured a deep 'waterslide' sidepod design that aggressively channeled air to the rear diffuser.

cars.name: McLaren MCL60
cars.description: Named to celebrate McLaren's 60th anniversary, it started the season terribly slow. However, a miraculous mid-season upgrade package transformed it into one of the fastest cars on the grid.
cars.funFact: Rookie Oscar Piastri managed to win a Sprint race in Qatar driving the heavily upgraded MCL60.

cars.name: Williams FW45
cars.description: The FW45 was notorious for its incredibly slippery aerodynamics. It lacked downforce but was an absolute rocket ship on the straights, allowing Alex Albon to defend brilliantly at tracks like Monza and Canada.
cars.funFact: The car was so fast on the straights that rivals often found it impossible to overtake even with their DRS open.

cars.name: Ferrari SF-24
cars.description: The SF-24 cured the unpredictable handling of its predecessor, providing a stable platform that was much kinder to its tires. Carlos Sainz used it to win the Australian GP just weeks after appendicitis surgery.
cars.funFact: Charles Leclerc finally broke his 'home curse' by winning the Monaco Grand Prix in this car.

cars.name: McLaren MCL38
cars.description: An evolution of the MCL60, the MCL38 proved to be an incredibly versatile and fast car across all track types. Lando Norris took his maiden F1 victory in Miami after a well-timed safety car.
cars.funFact: The car's ability to generate high-speed downforce allowed McLaren to challenge Red Bull's dominance consistently.

cars.name: Mercedes W15
cars.description: The W15 finally abandoned the failed 'zero-pod' concept entirely. After a tricky start, upgrades unlocked its potential, allowing Lewis Hamilton to win an emotional British Grand Prix, his first win in 945 days.
cars.funFact: The car featured a unique trick front wing flap designed to legally mimic the airflow effect of a banned vortex generator.
